speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 15th, 1996. I'm interviewing Margaret Pappenheimer Lowe. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. We are in Merrick, New York, USA. And the language is English. What's today? The 15th?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 15th, 1996. I'm interviewing Margaret Pappenheimer Lowe. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. We're in Merrick, New York. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Judith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your name, and say it and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margaret Lowe, nee Pappenheimer. M-A-R-G-A-R-E-T L-O-W-E. And my maiden name is Pappenheimer, P-A-P-P-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born September 12th, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: We start the interview with you telling me about your family, when you were born, when you were a little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Germany. I was an only child. And my parents' name was Martha Pappenheimer. Her maiden name was Gutmann. And my father's name was David Pappenheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my parents were not so young anymore when I-- when they were married and I was born, because there was a World War. My father was in the army and was a soldier. And he was the oldest son of-- and his mother was widowed, so he married rather late. I guess he was 40 years old. And-- and my mother was born in Bruchsal in Germany. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I was born, I guess she was 30. And I grew up in-- in Germany, in Munich. And my father was a businessman there. And-- and my mother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we lived in-- do you want to know the address? Aussere Prinzregentenstrasse 23 in Munich. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what else can I tell you? Your father was a businessman? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had a business of wholesale-- wholesale grocery, I guess you would call it. And they supplied retail grocery businesses and restaurants. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in business by himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: With his cousin, his cousin. He had a cousin. They were partners in the business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before-- before my father married, he was born in-- in a small town called Oberdorf in Wuerttemberg. And he was the-- the oldest of-- no, he was not the oldest. He was the second oldest, but he was the oldest son. And there were four children. And my mother had two siblings. And she was born, as I said, in Bruchsal. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had an older sister and a younger brother. I didn't know my cousins, because they all lived in different cities. And so I, you know, I didn't see them very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your father was in the war, that's why he got married late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was the First World War. My father was-- was in the army and was wounded several times with all-- you know, in one-- in all these battles that you nowadays hear about in history lessons. And-- and then he-- after the war, he moved to Munich and went into partnership with his-- with his cousin. Before he was married, he traveled extensively all over, you know, the Middle East and Italy and Greece. And I have pictures showing some of his travels. After-- after what's-- after the war, of course, there were very bad times already in Germany with the inflation. And-- and there were housing shortages. And my father owned or bought this house where we lived. It was an apartment house. But nevertheless, they couldn't live in it 'til it became-- an apartment became vacant, because all this was-- you know, with shortages. And it was all regulated. And later on we lived-- when I was born, we lived in this apartment on the first floor in that apartment house. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my-- a short time, my grandparents-- or my grandfather after my grandmother had died, my grandfather lived in two rooms in the back of our apartment. But the-- my last grandparent died when I was four years old, so I have really no memory of-- of any grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When Hitler came to power was-- by that time I was-- well, he came to power in 1933, so I was 11 years old. But after that, of course, business and everything became rather difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I go back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what it was like when you were-- when you do remember first memories about what kind of neighborhood the apartment was in and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, let me think. I-- the apartment was in a outer suburb of Munich. It was just a few more houses. It was really an outer suburb. And at the end of the-- of the road was Franciscan monastery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going forward later in life, when the monks used to come back, the friars, we children, when we were playing in the street, everybody would stop and shake hands and curtsy to the friars. So in the time of Hitler, I would hang back when the other children-- and I remember an incident when one of the monks would call me from the-- from the doorway where-- and why don't you come and greet me like the other children? And I said, because I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess he meant to be kind. And he said, well, you can still come and greet me. But I was scared, you see. So that-- so as I said, we lived on the outskirts of town. I went to a grade school 'til I was-- from 6 to 10. And we had to walk across, again, in the outskirts. And the other-- some children came from outlying farms. They walked across the fields. And that there, that was the grade school I was at. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: With Jewish and--

speaker: interviewee
text: With Jewish children. Yeah. What I was going to say, for the Jewish children they had a Jewish teacher come in just for, you know, religion. Munich was a Catholic city. And the only other religion as far as I know was Catholic. So when-- twice a week we had religious classes. And there was a Jewish teacher who instructed the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to be separated from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think at-- at that time, in the early years, that bothered us any way, you know, We-- we just went into the separate classrooms and had the separate instruction. And, you know, in Germany was not separation of-- of religion and state. And so that was just part of-- of the lessons we learned. And the same continued later on in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the community where you were living was a mixed community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, the Jewish community was much-- you know, was small.

speaker: interviewer
text: How small?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- I-- you know, I guess they were not concentrated in-- certainly not where I lived in one area, so there were Jewish and non-Jewish children. And I think in the early years, we probably inter-mixed quite freely. But when I think of it, I guess the children that I visited with or saw were probably mostly Jewish children. But I did have-- now I'm thinking I did have non-Jewish friends that came to the house [BACKGROUND NOISE] too, you know, to play. And the children that when we played, you know, street games or so, they were mostly non-Jewish, the neighbors' children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family, in terms of their-- their religious affiliation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- well, we went to the high holidays. We went to synagogue. We went to the main synagogue in Munich, which was in the city, and mainly only on the religious-- on the high holidays. Later on when my father didn't work anymore towards the end, he used to go Saturday morning services. I believe that my father probably came from a more religious background than my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, we didn't keep a kosher home or anything like that. But I had very-- I mean, we had very good religious instruction, because we had very good teachers, apart-- you know, the history and the learning Hebrew, apart from keeping the-- you know, the Orthodox rules, which we didn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was school for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I was never a good student. I was very immature, I think. I was small in stature. And I was always-- we were always lined up according to height. And opposite to what they do in America, the little ones were in the back instead of in the front, so I was always in the back. And I wasn't very sporty, so all these things didn't make school that terribly happy. Later on in high school, I-- I was good, I guess, in math and physics. But I-- some teachers were already-- first of all, I was very fidgety child and I would always talk. And also, some of the teachers were already-- you know, there were some anti-Semitic teachers. I remember-- I mean, I wasn't brought up in a way that you come home and there you have a bad mark, because-- I mean, that wasn't done, absolutely not. I-- but I remember in the last year of high school, we had cooking lessons, because at that time it was-- yeah, we had cooking as a related subject. And we cooked a meal. And then we had to learn to serve and do all this. And at the end, a dispute broke out about who had to do the cleaning up. And it became-- I was the only Jewish child, and it became a confrontation. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt I was in the right and I spoke up. And the teacher disappeared into another room. She didn't want to get involved. You see, they were afraid already. And one or two others of my co-students backed me, you know. But the teacher, it was definitely by that time, them and us thing. And as I said, I was the only Jewish child. But I-- I spoke up, but the teacher did not defend me. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I had a couple-- I spoke up and I was sort of cornered. I don't-- I guess I had one or two other students that took my side. And-- but I'm only saying that the teachers certainly were not going to-- to get into-- you know, to protect a Jewish child in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the teacher say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She just [LAUGHS] made-- disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think we had really very often-- you know, there were no overt things. I was still only in school because my father was-- what's it called?-- a frontsoldat. That means he was-- had fought in the First War at the front. And when the-- the restrictions against Jews came up slowly. They didn't, you know, come overnight. They crept up. And about the last ones to made-- you know, to be affected were the children of the front-- the soldiers that had been at the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was actually a bad experience, because I think it lulled my parents and others into not making aggressive steps to leave. And everybody thought, you know, we'll all survive the Hitler time. Did your parents realize that the reason that you were still able to be in school was because of his wartime experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course, because all the new laws were enacted. And there will always then certain exceptions. And of course my parents realized it. But they did not-- you know, they all didn't realize what it would evolve into eventually. And when they did realize it, it was pretty late. I know-- I don't know if you have any other-- any question-- I-- I lived in-- in Munich across the street from Hitler. And so we saw certain things evolve. And in the early days, I mean, we used to cross the street. And at one time Hitler walked out. And-- where the-- the other people saluted with their hands stretched out, he always saluted like this. And he greeted my mother and me, because we just had the misfortune to cross the street as he came out of his house. And we, of course, just walked by and ignored it. And later on, of course, there was a little more-- what-- I mean, he always had the limousine waiting outside the house. And it used to wait there for hours. But in the early years, he would just walk out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the children-- when he came out, he came out with his little dog. He had a little-- well, we called it a fox terrier. I think here you call them wire-hair terrier. And he always had a whip in his hand, like just, you know, like a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not really that he was whipping anybody at that time. But-- and he would go to the limousine with his little dog. But the children came across and he would hand out oranges to the children, because oranges-- Germany had no foreign exchange, so oranges that came from Italy or Africa were a rare commodity. So the children used to walk-- run across and he would hand out oranges. And I used to go and run across too. But I didn't take any oranges. I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew I was a Jewish child. I hang-- you know, I held to the back. I wouldn't-- but, you know, just ran with the rest of the group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the story, I'm-- even why I'm telling this, because the proximity to Hitler is what, I think, I undid my parents in the end. But before I get to this, so as Hitler became-- you know, had more power and more power, all the other dignitaries also had houses. They had what we called villa, like a country home. And in the streets that I had to walk down to go to school, there was Himmler, who was known for-- he was Minister of-- no, that was Goebbels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Himmler was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] troopers, the black-- you know, the black troopers. What do you call it? The SS, the stormtrooper, head of. And there was Stuermer and Roehm, who was-- and they all had their houses along this row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Roehm was assassinated by the Nazi themselves, I think for political reasons, or also because it was later said he was homosexual. But there was also talk that he had planned to overthrow, some kind of a putsch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, so when-- when Hitler came out from the house, by later on, of course, the limousine waited. And then people started-- and the women were the most fanatic. There were thousands of women lined up on our side of the street waiting. And then, of course, they had barricades and so on that you could hardly walk by, waiting for hours for Hitler to come out. And the women were the most fanatic. The women really were, I guess, the most fanatic supporters.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware that it was Hitler who was living across the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess we always knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I guess when-- I don't know when he moved. You see, the historical thing is that-- I guess the putsch was-- now I'm not sure if it was 1922 or 1923. There was the first attempt to overthrow the then Bavarian government. And Hitler was at that time imprisoned. And that's when he wrote this Anyways, so later on, when he came out, I guess he held meetings-- Munich was, I guess, the seed bed of this whole thing. And he held meetings, because I remember our maid coming back from a meeting in the-- in not the pour houses, like in the pubs or so where he-- you know, it was very bad times and he promised better times. And-- and eventually, Hindenburg, who was president, made him the chancellor. And he was-- I mean, Hindenburg was an old, senile man already. So then when he had power-- but then he lived there already, so I don't know when he moved in, actually. In his very early years, there was another house in the inner city. But when-- I really don't know when [LAUGHS] he moved in. And unfortunately, I left before him. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he living there by himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I knew. But little by little, the house was taken over, I guess, with more of the Party dignitaries or whatever else. And when-- when [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Chamberlain came, when they-- to sign what they thought was then 'peace in our time,' they all came in-- in the motorcade. You know, they signed it because there was-- in the Party headquarters, which was in Munich in another location. And then the motorcade came to his private home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the motorcade. I was on my way home from school on the bicycle, so I saw the motorcade go by. But, you know, you couldn't see anybody who was in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize-- did you know what-- what it was and who it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Of course. And I think another thing is-- I mean, I'm now trying just to say the historical things-- another thing is when-- when Austria was taken over, we were up all night or we woke up in the night, because all the troop transports came through our street on the way to invade-- during the night they invaded Austria. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they come through your street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the road out down south, I guess one of the roads out of town. And this-- these lorries were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were thundering by, you know, woke us up. And what I'm trying to say, there was always a sense of unease, because you knew something was happening. We didn't hear 'til the next morning that Austria had been taken over. But all night these troop transports went on and on and on. And in those-- that was in 1938, I believe. It was always-- you lived with a sense of unease, something was happening always. And, you know, it was always ominous, nothing good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think Hitler knew that you, as a Jewish family, were living across the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I mean, I don't think so. I mean, we were not the only Jewish family in the street. But it was we were the most immediately across, I guess. There were others in other houses or so. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, it wasn't very noteworthy one way or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family make-- your mother and father comment about the fact that he was living across the street when you began to know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to watch when he had his birthday parties. We used to watch with binoculars. You could look into the-- you know, into his rooms. So that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you looked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember. I only know we looked. I guess presents were brought in and stuff like that. But I mean, the only reason it's of any importance is that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first of all, that we saw things and were-- also, the house had to be-- later on, they had to be secured, you know. And we had to put pails of-- in the attic space, pails of sand. I guess they were preparing, you know, for war or for bombing. And also, of course, we always-- my parents owned the house. And there were I guess 11 apartments. And-- and we had to always-- on the holidays, we had to put flags out. And of course, my parents, also being Jewish, they still had to put-- you know, buy the flags and put out the appropriate flags, which originally was German flag or Bavarian flag. But I think in the end they had to put out a Nazi flag too. You know, that was sort of mandated. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I've come to a halt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents had other people living in the apartment that were-- that were renting [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, yes. I mean, there were other apartment-- other apartments in the building. But later on, when my-- when my father's business began to suffer from the times, there were two rooms in the back that my mother then rented. And we had-- we had people renting there and became quite friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Margaret-- Margaret, I'm-- I'm going to stop you now, because we need to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
